WEBSTER

pages.raw_text:
the further fact that the unions
circulated cards giving the names
of the fair houses and urging all
friends of labor to patronize them,
did not constitute facts sufficient
to warrant the issue of an injunc-
ton against the strikers.
The plaintiffs charged the exist
ence of conspiracy among the de-
PENDANTS to ruin the business of
the plaintiffs, but the court held
that the evidence wholly failed to
support the conspiracy charge. The
only acts, the court held, that the
plaintiffs established, were the dis
tribution of the cards and the fact
that one union girl had talked to a
nonunion girl, in a peaceable and
orderly manner. The court then
denied the application for an in
junction against the culinary work
ers.


In passing on the case, Judge
Webster said. among other things:
"The rules of law as they relate to
strikes are well established in this
country and practically have been
settled. These rules are not hard
to apply to this case.


"The right of the workers to
strike is conceded, also the right by
peaceful and lawful means to per-
suade others to do the same.
Strikers have no right, however to
resort to threats, intimidation or
violence In seeking others to unite
forces with them. Any attempt at
force, intimidation or oppression to
coerce others into practices against
their free will is in violation Of the
law.


''In the present case tibet COM
plant Is good but the evidence to
support the allegations is lacking.
In the case of the Model bakery
the evidence shows that the two
girls met and had a friendly talk.
One tried by fair and lawful means
to get the other to see things as
she did. When she failed the girl
approached was permitted to go her
way and was not moltested. There
is no violation of law in this Incl
dent"
